headline: Iran begins burying Khamenei with his successor still in hiding, a Category-5 typhoon slams America's Pacific islands, and Mumbai shuts down under torrential rain — as diplomats converge on Ankara to talk about ending the Ukraine war.
the_big_picture: Today is a day of tests of who is actually in charge. Iran is staging the largest day of a week-long state funeral for its assassinated Supreme Leader while the son chosen to succeed him has not been seen in public since February — a superpower standoff with an invisible man at its centre. France finds out tomorrow whether its most popular far-right politician is allowed to run for president at all, and Donald Trump spent the weekend on the phone with both Putin and Zelensky ahead of a NATO summit that starts tomorrow in Ankara. Nature, meanwhile, is running its own stress tests: a Category-5 typhoon is crossing America's Pacific islands this morning, Mumbai has closed its schools after rain killed at least seven people, and Europe is fighting a culture war over air conditioning in record heat. For India it is an unusually consequential Monday — Modi begins a three-nation Indo-Pacific tour, the US case against Gautam Adani moves toward dismissal, and markets reopen to a calmer oil map than the one they feared a fortnight ago.

stories.title: Iran buries Khamenei — while the successor, his son, stays invisible
stories.background: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei ruled Iran for more than three decades as Supreme Leader — the country's highest religious and political authority, above the president. He was killed in a US-Israeli airstrike on 28 February, at the very start of this year's war between Iran and the US-Israel alliance. His 56-year-old son Mojtaba was hastily elected Supreme Leader in March, but he was reportedly badly wounded in the same attack (which also killed his mother and his wife) and has not appeared in public since — he communicates only through written statements.
stories.what_happened: Iran's week-long state funeral (3–9 July) reached its largest days, with millions expected in Tehran and processions planned through Qom and the Iraqi holy cities of Najaf and Karbala before burial in Mashhad. On Sunday three of Khamenei's sons prayed beside his coffin — but Mojtaba, the one who now holds his father's job, did not appear. The ceremony unfolded amid fresh rhetoric: Donald Trump warned Iran the US needs only 'one shot', and Tehran replied that America 'can kill people, but not ideals.'
stories.why_it_matters: A country of 90 million people, sitting on one of the world's biggest oil and gas reserves, is being led by a man nobody has seen for four months. That is an extraordinary vacuum at the top of a state that just fought a war with the United States and Israel. The funeral is also a political performance: the Islamic Republic is using religious imagery to project continuity and defiance at the exact moment its leadership looks most fragile.
stories.ripple_effects: If Mojtaba cannot rule visibly, factions inside Iran — the Revolutionary Guards, rival clerics — will compete to fill the space, and that competition could pull Iran toward either accommodation with the West or renewed confrontation. Every neighbour, oil trader and foreign ministry is watching the same question: who actually gives orders in Tehran? The answer determines whether the fragile ceasefire and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz (the sea lane carrying about a fifth of the world's oil) hold.
stories.why_now: The funeral was delayed more than four months by the war and mourning rules; this week's ceremonies are the regime's first big set-piece since the ceasefire — and Mojtaba's absence from his own father's funeral turned a memorial into a succession mystery.
stories.watch_next: Whether Mojtaba Khamenei appears (or even speaks) before the burial in Mashhad on 9 July — and how Washington responds if he does not.

key_terms.definition: Iran's highest authority — a cleric who outranks the elected president and controls the military, judiciary and state media.
An agreed halt to fighting that stops short of a formal peace deal — it can collapse if either side stops honouring it.

stories.the_lesson: In personalised political systems, the person IS the institution — when a leader dies or disappears, the uncertainty isn't just about one job but about whether the whole system can still make decisions. Markets and rivals price that uncertainty immediately.
stories.market_link: Any sign of chaos or hardline consolidation in Tehran would put a risk premium back into oil (WTI sits oversold near $68.78) — a direct concern for oil-importer India.

stories.title: Super Typhoon Bavi, a Category-5 monster, crosses Guam and the Marianas today
stories.background: Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands are small US territories in the western Pacific, home to about 220,000 people and major American military bases. The region is typhoon country, but storms of the very top category — 5, meaning sustained winds above 157 mph — are rare direct hits. Bavi is already the third Category-5 storm of 2026, a pace scientists link to unusually warm ocean water, which is the fuel typhoons feed on.
stories.what_happened: Super Typhoon Bavi is making its closest pass over the islands this Monday morning local time, with forecast landfall-strength winds around 165 mph (265+ km/h), gusts well above that, waves up to 11 metres (35 feet), and 300–500 mm of rain — most of it inside a single 12-hour window. Evacuations were ordered across Guam, and the most likely impact zone runs between Guam and the islands of Tinian and Saipan.
stories.why_it_matters: Winds at Category-5 strength don't just damage buildings — they can destroy water, power and communications for weeks on islands that can't easily be resupplied. These islands were already battered by typhoons earlier this season, so this is a compounding disaster: infrastructure weakened once is hit again before it has healed.
stories.ripple_effects: The US military's key western-Pacific hub may be degraded for a period. After the Marianas, forecasts steer Bavi toward Taiwan by around 11 July — putting the world's most important semiconductor-manufacturing island on storm watch next. Insurance costs for Pacific territories keep ratcheting up, a quiet tax that climate change levies on island life.
stories.why_now: Peak Pacific typhoon season plus record-warm sea surface temperatures — the same warm-ocean signal behind this year's unusual number of top-category storms.
stories.watch_next: Damage reports from Guam and Saipan over the next 24 hours, and whether Bavi holds its strength on the forecast track toward Taiwan (~11 July).

key_terms.definition: The top of the hurricane/typhoon scale — sustained winds above 157 mph, capable of catastrophic destruction.
Seawater pushed ashore by a storm's winds and low pressure — often the deadliest part of a typhoon, not the wind itself.

stories.the_lesson: Warm oceans are storm fuel — as seas heat up, the strongest storm categories become more common, and places hit repeatedly suffer more than the sum of each hit because recovery never finishes. Disasters compound.
stories.market_link: Minimal direct market impact today, but if Bavi tracks into Taiwan next weekend it becomes a watch item for chipmakers and their supply chains.

stories.title: Mumbai shuts schools as monsoon fury kills at least seven — a chawl collapses in Mankhurd
stories.background: Mumbai's monsoon (June–September) is both a lifeline and an annual hazard: the city depends on the rains, but its drainage, built for a smaller city, is overwhelmed when 200+ mm falls in a day. 'Chawls' are Mumbai's old, low-cost multi-storey housing blocks — single-room tenements with shared balconies — many of them decades old and structurally fragile, housing some of the city's poorest residents.
stories.what_happened: Torrential rain battered Mumbai and the wider Maharashtra coast on Sunday. A multi-storey chawl collapsed in Mankhurd (which recorded ~134 mm of rain), killing at least six people; a 63-year-old man died when a tree fell on a shop in Kurla. Several areas crossed 200 mm of rain under an IMD red alert, flight operations at Mumbai airport were halted for about an hour, and the city has declared a holiday for all schools and colleges today (Monday, 6 July) as the IMD keeps an orange alert in place.
stories.why_it_matters: For India's financial capital, this is the season's first full-scale stress test — and the deaths show the human cost concentrates in the oldest, poorest housing. Beyond Mumbai, how the monsoon behaves matters to the whole economy: it decides crop output, rural incomes and food prices for hundreds of millions.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect disrupted commutes, flight delays and possible fresh building-safety orders in Mumbai this week. Economically, a strong monsoon is usually good news for India (it waters the kharif — summer-sown — crop), but destructive cloudbursts in cities cut activity and can damage goods and infrastructure. If heavy rain persists, watch vegetable prices — the fastest way weather becomes inflation.
stories.why_now: Peak monsoon onset over the Konkan coast; the IMD had a red alert on Mumbai through the weekend and has kept an orange alert for today.
stories.watch_next: Whether the IMD extends alerts beyond Monday, and the structural-safety response to the Mankhurd chawl collapse.

key_terms.definition: An old Mumbai housing block of single-room tenements with shared corridors — cheap, crowded and often structurally weak.
India's weather-warning scale: orange means 'be prepared' for very heavy rain; red means 'take action' — extremely heavy rain likely.

stories.the_lesson: Extreme rain doesn't strike a city evenly — it finds the weakest structures and the poorest neighbourhoods first. Climate resilience is really a housing and drainage story, and the monsoon is as much an economic event as a weather event.
stories.market_link: A monsoon that turns destructive (or deficient) feeds food inflation, which shapes RBI rate policy — one of the swing factors for Indian markets this quarter.

stories.title: Trump works the phones with Putin and Zelensky ahead of tomorrow's NATO summit
stories.background: Russia's full-scale war on Ukraine is in its fifth year, but the battlefield balance has been shifting: Ukraine has demonstrated it can strike military and energy targets deep inside Russia (it burned a major Russian oil terminal last week), eroding what Moscow assumed was a safe rear. Donald Trump has repeatedly offered himself as the dealmaker who ends the war; his envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner have been shuttling toward Moscow for months.
stories.what_happened: Trump held a roughly 90-minute call with Vladimir Putin on 4 July — described by Kremlin aide Yury Ushakov as 'businesslike and quite constructive' — in which Trump offered to help find a settlement; the Kremlin said it seeks a 'political-diplomatic resolution.' Trump also spoke with Volodymyr Zelensky, who called it 'a very good call' and said 'there is a real prospect to put an end to this war.' All of this lands ahead of the NATO summit in Ankara on 7–8 July.
stories.why_it_matters: This is the most explicit peace choreography in months, and it is happening because the leverage has moved: Russia's energy infrastructure is now vulnerable, its ally Iran is weakened, and both sides have reasons to test a deal. Whether talk becomes negotiation depends on the terms — territory, security guarantees for Ukraine, and sanctions relief for Russia are the hard parts no phone call resolves.
stories.ripple_effects: A genuine peace track would reshape energy markets (Russian oil and gas flows), European defence budgets, and grain trade. A failed one could mean escalation instead — Ukraine has new deep-strike reach, and Russia will be tempted to answer. For India, which buys discounted Russian crude, any settlement that normalises Russian oil would quietly rearrange its energy bill.
stories.why_now: The call was timed just before the Ankara NATO summit — Trump wants to arrive with momentum, and both Moscow and Kyiv want to shape the agenda rather than react to it.
stories.watch_next: The NATO summit in Ankara on 7–8 July: whether it produces a concrete negotiating framework or just communiqués.

key_terms.definition: The Western military alliance of 32 countries built on collective defence — an attack on one member is treated as an attack on all.
A binding promise by outside powers to defend a country if attacked — the core of what Ukraine wants from any peace deal.

stories.the_lesson: Wars move toward negotiation when the battlefield leverage shifts, not when the talking starts — diplomacy usually ratifies facts created on the ground rather than replacing them.
stories.market_link: Peace prospects press on oil and European gas prices, lift European equities, and would weigh on defence stocks; failure does the reverse.

stories.title: The Adani case heads for dismissal — but a US judge wants answers by 13 July
stories.background: In 2024, US prosecutors under the Biden administration indicted Gautam Adani — one of India's two richest industrialists — and seven associates, alleging a scheme to bribe Indian officials with about $250 million to win solar-power contracts while raising money from US investors. Adani has always denied wrongdoing. In May 2026 the Trump-era Department of Justice moved to drop the case entirely; the change of administration changed the case's fate.
stories.what_happened: The DoJ has formally urged the court to dismiss the charges permanently, saying the case 'should never have been brought': the conduct happened almost entirely in India, Indian authorities found no actionable misconduct, investors lost no money, and the evidence sits abroad — adding that acting as the 'world police' can cause diplomatic strife. But District Judge Nicholas Garaufis called the DoJ's explanation 'terse, bland and conclusory' and ordered a fuller justification by 13 July. US media reports note Adani hired Robert Giuffra Jr — one of Trump's personal lawyers — and that his team floated a $10-billion US investment pledge while lobbying the DoJ; the DoJ says the decision was not tied to investments.
stories.why_it_matters: For India's markets and business establishment, the near-certain end of this case removes a cloud that has hung over the Adani group — ports, airports, power, cement — for two years. For observers of US justice, it is a test case of whether prosecutorial decisions now track political relationships: the reported investment offer and the Trump-linked legal team are exactly why the judge is demanding a paper trail.
stories.ripple_effects: Adani group companies could re-rate (investors paying more per rupee of earnings) as legal risk fades, and the group's access to global capital reopens. Diplomatically, it clears an irritant in US-India relations at a moment both sides are negotiating on trade. The judge's demand keeps a small chance alive that the dismissal comes with conditions or embarrassing disclosures.
stories.why_now: The DoJ's detailed justification is due to Judge Garaufis by 13 July — this week decides whether the dismissal is rubber-stamped or scrutinised.
stories.watch_next: The DoJ's 13 July filing and whether the judge grants dismissal 'with prejudice' (permanently, so charges can't be refiled).

key_terms.definition: A formal criminal charge — an accusation to be tested at trial, not a finding of guilt.
Ending a case permanently, so the same charges cannot be brought again.

stories.the_lesson: For global companies, legal risk lives at the intersection of law and politics — a change of government can change a case's fate, which is why markets price 'jurisdiction risk' and why judges demand reasons when prosecutors reverse course.
stories.market_link: Adani group stocks are the direct play; more broadly it supports foreign-investor sentiment toward Indian large-caps.

stories.title: France learns tomorrow whether Le Pen can run for president
stories.background: Marine Le Pen has led France's far right for over a decade and came closer to the presidency with each of her three runs. In 2025 a court convicted her of embezzling European Parliament funds — using EU money meant for parliamentary assistants to pay party staff — and, crucially, barred her from public office. She appealed; without a reversal she cannot contest the 2027 election, widely seen as her best-ever chance to succeed Emmanuel Macron.
stories.what_happened: The Paris Court of Appeal delivers its ruling on Tuesday, 7 July. Prosecutors have recommended a four-year sentence (one year to be served, possibly under electronic monitoring), a €100,000 fine — and a five-year ban on holding public office, which is the part that would end her 2027 candidacy.
stories.why_it_matters: This is one court decision that could redraw European politics: polls have long placed Le Pen among the favourites for 2027. If she is barred, her 30-year-old protégé Jordan Bardella likely becomes the far right's candidate — younger, less tested, but currently popular. The case also sharpens a debate democracies keep having: courts enforcing the law against politicians versus voters' right to choose whom they want.
stories.ripple_effects: A ban would trigger accusations of political justice and could energise her base behind Bardella; an acquittal instantly makes her the 2027 frontrunner and unsettles mainstream parties. Investors watch French elections closely because far-right fiscal plans have previously rattled the bond market — the market where governments borrow.
stories.why_now: The appeals court set 7 July as its ruling date back in February; the decision lands tomorrow.
stories.watch_next: Tuesday's ruling — specifically whether the public-office ban is upheld, softened, or overturned.

key_terms.definition: A higher court that re-examines a conviction or sentence — it can uphold, change or cancel the original decision.
A legal bar on holding or running for public office, sometimes imposed as part of a criminal sentence.

stories.the_lesson: In modern democracies, courtrooms can matter as much as ballot boxes — a single legal ruling can remove a frontrunner and reroute a country's politics, which is why 'political risk' includes judicial calendars.
stories.market_link: French government bonds and the euro are sensitive to any outcome that raises the odds of a far-right, high-spending presidency in 2027.

stories.title: America's jobs engine is sputtering — and this week the new Fed chair shows his cards
stories.background: The US central bank (the Federal Reserve) sets interest rates to balance inflation against jobs; the whole world watches because dollar rates set the price of money everywhere. The Fed has been holding rates high to fight inflation that is still running near 4.2%. Kevin Warsh recently took over as Fed chair — and he has already signalled he dislikes central-bank forecasting rituals like the 'dot plot' of rate projections.
stories.what_happened: June's jobs report showed the US added just 57,000 jobs — about half the ~115,000 expected. The unemployment rate FELL to 4.2%, but for a bad reason: so many people stopped looking for work that labour-force participation hit its lowest level in 50 years outside the Covid era (you only count as unemployed if you're actively searching). This week Wall Street returns from the long July-4th weekend to a light calendar whose main event is Wednesday's minutes from the June Fed meeting — the first under Warsh.
stories.why_it_matters: Weak hiring kills the fear of further rate HIKES — that relief is what pushed the Dow to a record last week. But sticky ~4.2% inflation means no cuts either: the Fed is stuck 'higher for longer.' The deeper worry is what falling participation says — an economy where people give up looking for work is cooling in a way a single unemployment number hides.
stories.ripple_effects: For the US: cheaper borrowing hopes stay parked, and a slowing consumer eventually hits company profits. For India: a Fed on hold keeps the dollar firm and the rupee under pressure near record lows (~95 per dollar), and a cooling US consumer is a slow headwind for Indian IT and export earnings. Gold — which pays no interest and so shines when rate rises fade — has stayed firm near $4,187.
stories.why_now: Markets reopen today after the holiday; Wednesday's Fed minutes are the first written window into how the Warsh-era Fed actually reasons.
stories.watch_next: Wednesday's June Fed minutes, then the 29 July Fed decision — and whether the next US inflation print revives hike fears or opens the door to cuts.

key_terms.definition: The share of adults either working or actively job-hunting — it can fall (and flatter the unemployment rate) when discouraged people simply stop looking.
The detailed written record of a Federal Reserve meeting, released three weeks later — traders mine it for clues about future rate moves.

stories.the_lesson: Never read one number alone: the unemployment rate can 'improve' because people gave up, not because they found jobs. Participation tells you whether a falling jobless rate is good news or a warning.
stories.market_link: Rates-sensitive everything: US stocks and gold ride the 'no more hikes' relief; the firm dollar keeps the rupee near record lows and pressures foreign flows into India.

stories.title: Modi begins a three-nation Indo-Pacific tour today — including the first PM visit to New Zealand in ~40 years
stories.background: India's 'Act East' policy is its long-running effort to deepen trade and security ties across Southeast Asia and the Pacific, largely as a counterweight to China's growing naval and economic reach. India signed a free-trade agreement with New Zealand in April 2026 — one of its fastest-negotiated deals — and holds annual summits with Australia, a fellow member of the Quad security grouping.
stories.what_happened: Prime Minister Modi departs today on a three-nation tour: Indonesia (6–8 July, with talks in Jakarta and a visit to the Prambanan temple complex in Yogyakarta), Australia (8–10 July, including the third India-Australia Annual Summit in Melbourne), and New Zealand (10–11 July) — the first visit by an Indian prime minister in nearly four decades. The agenda centres on maritime security, defence, trade, investment and technology.
stories.why_it_matters: This is India positioning itself as the Indo-Pacific's swing power. Indonesia is Southeast Asia's biggest economy and guards key sea lanes; Australia is a defence partner and critical-minerals supplier; New Zealand is a fresh FTA partner. Together the trip stitches India's economic and security webs across the region — with China's shadow the unspoken reason everyone is eager to host.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect deliverables in defence cooperation, shipping and ports, critical minerals (Australia supplies inputs for batteries and chips), and diaspora-friendly measures. For businesses, summit outcomes often translate into market access and investment corridors — the India-NZ FTA due for implementation later this year is the concrete example.
stories.why_now: The tour starts today; the Melbourne summit is a scheduled annual fixture, and India wants momentum on regional trade while its deals with the UK (live 15 July) and New Zealand come on stream.
stories.watch_next: Concrete agreements out of the Melbourne India-Australia summit (8–10 July) — especially anything on defence, minerals or trade.

key_terms.definition: The strategic region spanning the Indian and Pacific Oceans — the arena where the US, China, India and allies compete for influence and secure shipping lanes.
A pact between countries that cuts tariffs (import taxes) and eases rules so more goods and services can flow between them.

stories.the_lesson: Middle powers don't pick one side — they build overlapping webs of trade and security partnerships, so no single relationship can hold them hostage. Diplomacy is diversification.
stories.market_link: Watch export-linked Indian sectors (defence, engineering, dairy/agri around the NZ FTA) for summit-driven news; the trade-pact drumbeat supports sentiment more than any single stock.

stories.title: Record heat scorches two continents — and Europe starts a culture war over air conditioning
stories.background: Europe has historically lived without air conditioning — mild summers made it unnecessary, and energy-conscious culture made it unfashionable. That assumption is dying: June temperature records were smashed across the continent (the UK broke its June record at 37.7C), and France logged 2,025 excess deaths at the peak of its last heatwave. In the US, a dangerous heat dome sat over the July-4th holiday.
stories.what_happened: Germany hit unprecedented highs — 41.7C in Brandenburg on Sunday — as another heatwave builds; the UK issued a week-long health alert with temperatures heading for 34C. The scramble to cool down has turned political: a Guardian report describes an 'AC culture war' over whether air conditioning is a lifesaver or an energy-hogging surrender, while US heat advisories put the heat index at ~106°F in the Mid-Atlantic. One trade-war twist: a historic heatwave has driven record European demand for Chinese-made air conditioners — just as Brussels tries to rebalance trade with Beijing.
stories.why_it_matters: Heat is now Europe's deadliest weather — those 2,025 French excess deaths are a bigger toll than most storms — and the infrastructure (grids, homes, hospitals, railways) was built for a climate that no longer exists. The AC debate is really a debate about adaptation: cooling saves lives but strains power grids and raises emissions unless the electricity is clean.
stories.ripple_effects: Electricity demand spikes are stressing grids on both continents (the US Department of Energy even deleted thousands of energy-conservation web pages amid a political row over asking people to raise their thermostats). Sustained heat lifts power prices, cuts outdoor-work productivity, stresses crops, and — via the AC import surge — hands China more leverage in EU trade talks.
stories.why_now: Peak Northern-Hemisphere summer, with back-to-back heatwaves compressing what used to be once-a-decade extremes into successive weeks.
stories.watch_next: Whether this week's European heatwave breaks national July records — and how grid operators cope with record cooling demand.

key_terms.definition: Deaths above the normal expected number for a period — the standard way to count a heatwave's true toll, since heat rarely appears on death certificates.
What the temperature 'feels like' once humidity is added — high humidity stops sweat from cooling you, making the same temperature more dangerous.

stories.the_lesson: Climate change shows up as an infrastructure bill: societies built for yesterday's weather must now retrofit grids, homes and habits — and who pays for adaptation becomes a political fight everywhere.
stories.market_link: Heatwaves lift power prices and utilities' load, boost cooling-appliance makers, and add a weather premium to European energy markets.

stories.title: India's E20 petrol backlash grows: most drivers surveyed say mileage has dropped
stories.background: India blends ethanol (an alcohol fuel distilled mostly from sugarcane and grain) into petrol to cut its huge oil-import bill, support farmers, and lower emissions. The blend has been ratcheted up over years to 'E20' — 20% ethanol — now the standard pump fuel. The catch: ethanol carries less energy per litre than petrol, and older engines weren't designed for it.
stories.what_happened: A LocalCircles survey found 53% of vehicle owners rate the E20 rollout negatively — many calling it 'disastrous' — and 66% report a drop in mileage (kilometres per litre). Automakers and analysts counter that E20 fuel is compliant and thoroughly tested, and manufacturers have described service protocols for affected vehicles. The row has been building for weeks as more drivers connect fuel-economy complaints to the blend.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a policy with real winners and losers: the country saves foreign exchange on crude imports and sugarcane farmers gain a market, but drivers of older vehicles may quietly pay via more frequent fill-ups — a hidden per-kilometre cost increase. Public trust in the transition depends on honest measurement of that trade-off, and the survey suggests trust is fraying.
stories.ripple_effects: Political pressure to slow the blending ramp or compensate consumers; scrutiny of fuel-economy claims; and commercial stakes for oil-marketing companies, sugar/ethanol producers and automakers (who must certify engines for E20). If mileage losses are real at scale, effective fuel demand rises slightly — partly offsetting the import savings the policy chases.
stories.why_now: The LocalCircles survey published Sunday gave the simmering complaints a number — and the industry pushed back the same weekend.
stories.watch_next: Whether the government orders an independent mileage study or adjusts the blending roadmap in response.

key_terms.definition: Petrol blended with 20% ethanol — cheaper and cleaner-burning per litre, but with less energy, which can mean fewer kilometres per litre.
How far a vehicle travels per litre of fuel — a small percentage drop compounds into real money over a year of driving.

stories.the_lesson: Every energy transition redistributes costs — a policy that saves a country money in aggregate can still cost individual households at the pump, and unmeasured trade-offs are where public backlash starts.
stories.market_link: Touches oil-marketing companies, sugar/ethanol stocks and automakers — and, at the margin, India's crude-import bill.

stories.title: EasyJet agrees in principle to a £5.5bn takeover — Britain's biggest budget airline goes private
stories.background: EasyJet is the UK's largest low-cost carrier, a listed company since 2000 and a staple of European travel. Castlelake, a US investment firm that specialises in aviation assets, has pursued it for months — easyJet's board rejected four earlier offers before this one.
stories.what_happened: On Sunday evening easyJet announced an 'agreement in principle' to accept Castlelake's £5.5bn offer, which would take the airline off the London stock market and into private ownership. Final terms still need to be formalised and approved by shareholders and regulators.
stories.why_it_matters: A household-name airline leaving the public market is part of a bigger pattern: private investment firms buying UK-listed companies they consider undervalued, shrinking the London stock exchange. For a 'smart but new' reader, this is what 'take-private' means — public shareholders are bought out at a premium, and the company's future plays out away from daily market scrutiny.
stories.ripple_effects: Other UK mid-cap companies get re-examined as takeover candidates, which can lift their share prices. For passengers little changes immediately, but private owners often push harder on costs and fleet strategy. For London's market, each departure renews the debate about why UK shares trade cheaply enough to attract these bids.
stories.why_now: After four rejected offers, Castlelake finally met the board's price — the announcement came Sunday evening to precede Monday's market open.
stories.watch_next: The formal offer document and shareholder vote — and whether any rival bidder emerges.

key_terms.definition: When an investor buys all of a listed company's shares and removes it from the stock exchange — public shareholders are paid out, usually at a premium.
A deal's outline that both sides intend to complete — key terms agreed, but not yet legally final.

stories.the_lesson: When a stock market persistently undervalues companies, private buyers step in to arbitrage the gap — a wave of take-privates is often the market's way of saying prices were too low.
stories.market_link: Airline and UK mid-cap stocks may firm as investors hunt the next takeover candidate.

stories.title: Scientists may have found how Alzheimer's spreads through the brain — and a way to block it
stories.background: Alzheimer's disease, the most common cause of dementia, affects tens of millions of people worldwide, and every family touched by it knows the pattern: it starts with memory and spreads relentlessly. Scientists have long known that a protein called Tau turns toxic and accumulates in dying brain cells — but not how the damage travels from neuron to neuron.
stories.what_happened: New research suggests a common brain protein acts as an unexpected courier, packaging toxic Tau proteins and carrying them from damaged neurons into healthy ones — effectively the disease's delivery service. In lab work, blocking these harmful protein packages before they reached new cells stopped the spread, pointing to a possible way to slow the disease's progression.
stories.why_it_matters: Most Alzheimer's drugs so far try to clear the junk after it accumulates, with modest results. Identifying the transport mechanism gives medicine a different kind of target: interrupt the courier, and you might freeze the disease where it is rather than chase it after it spreads. It's early — cell and animal work, not a human therapy — but it's a genuinely new angle.
stories.ripple_effects: If the mechanism holds up in humans, it opens a new drug-development race and could eventually change how early-stage patients are treated — slowing progression matters enormously even without a cure, because Alzheimer's costs (care hours, lost independence) scale with severity.
stories.why_now: The findings were published in the past week and are gathering attention as one of the clearest mechanistic leads in years.
stories.watch_next: Whether the result replicates and moves toward human trials — the usual multi-year gap between mechanism and medicine.

key_terms.definition: A protein that normally stabilises brain-cell structure but, in Alzheimer's, misfolds into toxic tangles that kill neurons.
A brain cell that carries signals — the basic wiring of thought and memory.

stories.the_lesson: In medicine, knowing HOW a disease moves is as valuable as knowing what causes it — mechanisms are what drugs can actually target, which is why basic research keeps paying off decades later.

also_notable.title: NASA launches an emergency robotic mission to catch the falling Swift space telescope before it burns up
also_notable.why: A first-of-its-kind orbital rescue — a private spacecraft will try to boost a 22-year-old observatory back to safety.

also_notable.title: Australia win a record seventh Women's T20 World Cup, chasing the highest-ever final target against England
also_notable.why: Chased 151 with 17 balls to spare — the most dominant dynasty in cricket keeps extending its era.

also_notable.title: Sudan's war intensifies: drone strikes pummel the besieged city of El Obeid, killing students and civilians
also_notable.why: A largely unwatched humanitarian catastrophe keeps deepening as drones make sieges deadlier.

also_notable.title: Google's €4.1bn Android fine stands after it loses its final European appeal
also_notable.why: One of the biggest antitrust penalties ever is now final — a marker for how Europe polices Big Tech.

also_notable.title: Yemen flares again: officials say 50 Houthi fighters killed after a surprise offensive near Hodeidah
